channel.title: Quantum Crier
channel.description: Daily curated quantum computing news and insights by Mark Eatherly

item.title: On-chip Parametric Amplification in a Double Quantum Dots Circuit
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research addresses a critical bottleneck in quantum computing by developing an innovative on-chip amplifier that uses a single quantum dot as the amplification element, eliminating the need for bulky external amplifiers that are vulnerable to magnetic fields and suffer from signal loss. The team achieved an impressive 11 dB gain and demonstrated a two-fold improvement in signal-to-noise ratio when reading out quantum states, which directly translates to higher-fidelity quantum operations. What makes this particularly exciting is the potential for seamless integration into existing quantum circuits, as the amplifier uses the same double quantum dot technology already employed in many quantum computing architectures. This breakthrough could significantly simplify quantum computer designs while improving their performance, representing the kind of elegant engineering solution that helps move quantum computing from laboratory demonstrations toward practical systems.

In microwave-based quantum circuits, including double quantum dots (DQDs), superconducting qubits and spin qubits, parametric amplifiers are indispensable in achieving high-fidelity qubit readouts. Despite its importance, the application of parametric amplifiers is hampered by several challenges, such as high insertion losses, constrained tunability, and a pronounced vulnerability to magnetic fields. Here, we demonstrate an on-site single-atom parametric amplifier (SAPA) within a reconfigurable quantum circuit, which consists of a superconducting microwave cavity and two GaAs gate-defined DQDs. Leveraging the inherent nonlinearity of the DQD, a parametric gain exceeding 11 dB is achieved. This gain contributes to enhance the qubit readout, as evidenced by exceeding two times improvement in the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) when employing the DQD-based amplifier for reading out another DQD. Our work not only presents a versatile experimental platform with enhanced readout capabilities in quantum computing, but also introduces alternative choices of parametric amplifiers for a variety of microwave-based quantum circuits.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Barren Plateaus Beyond Observable Concentration
item.description:
Curator's Take: This arXiv paper tackles one of the most fundamental obstacles plaguing quantum machine learning: the dreaded barren plateau problem where training gradients vanish exponentially as quantum circuits grow larger. The researchers provide a crucial conceptual breakthrough by separating the tangled explanations for why these plateaus occur, distinguishing between simple "observable concentration" effects and more complex mid-circuit dynamics like information loss and scrambling that can kill gradients even when measurements behave well. This unified framework could be transformative for the field, as it gives quantum algorithm designers a clearer roadmap for avoiding the scaling pitfalls that have limited practical quantum machine learning applications. The identification of information-loss-induced barren plateaus in quantum convolutional networks particularly highlights how even seemingly well-designed architectures can fail in unexpected ways.

Parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs) are central to quantum machine learning and near-term quantum simulation, but their scalability is often hindered by barren plateaus (BPs), where gradients decay exponentially with system size. Prior explanations, including expressivity, entanglement, locality, and noise, are often presented in ways that conflate two distinct issues: concentration of the measured observable and loss of parameter sensitivity caused by circuit dynamics. We develop a unified statistical framework that separates these mechanisms. We show that several standard BP explanations, including locality- and entanglement-related effects, can be understood through a single phenomenon that we term observable concentration (OC). Importantly, we prove that avoiding OC is necessary but not sufficient for trainability. Beyond OC, we identify two distinct mid-circuit sources of gradient suppression. First, mid-circuit information loss occurs when parameter perturbations propagate into degrees of freedom that are inaccessible to the final measurement, yielding little or no response. Second, mid-circuit information scrambling occurs when local perturbations rapidly spread across the system and become effectively undetectable on the measured subsystem. We support our theory with explicit constructions and numerical evidence, including quantum convolutional neural network architectures that exhibit information-loss-induced barren plateaus despite the absence of observable concentration.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Simulating Quantum Error Correction beyond Pauli Stochastic Errors
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of the most critical blind spots in quantum error correction by developing tools to understand how real-world quantum computers will behave beyond the simplified error models typically used in theory. While most fault-tolerance research assumes nice, well-behaved Pauli errors that flip qubits randomly, actual quantum hardware produces messy coherent errors that can correlate across multiple qubits in unpredictable ways. The authors' new simulation framework reveals that these realistic errors can dramatically worsen performance, potentially shifting the famous fault-tolerance threshold and making magic state preparation an order of magnitude more expensive than expected. This work provides essential reality-checking tools for the quantum computing field as it transitions from proof-of-concept demonstrations to building truly fault-tolerant machines.

Quantum error correction (QEC), the lynchpin of fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC), is designed and validated against well-behaved Pauli stochastic error models. But in real-world deployment, QEC protocols encounter a vast array of other errors -- coherent and non-Pauli errors -- whose impacts on quantum circuits are vastly different than those of stochastic Pauli errors. The impacts of these errors on QEC and FTQC protocols have been largely unpredictable to date due to exponential classical simulation cost. Here, we show how to accurately and efficiently model the effects of coherent and non-Pauli errors on FTQC, and we study the effects of such errors on syndrome extraction for surface and bivariate bicycle codes, and on magic state cultivation. Our analysis suggests that coherent error can shift fault-tolerance thresholds, increase the space-time cost of magic state cultivation, and can increase logical error rates by an order of magnitude compared to equivalent stochastic errors. These analyses are enabled by a new technique for mapping any Markovian circuit-level error model with sufficiently small error rates onto a detector error model (DEM) for an FTQC circuit. The resulting DEM enables Monte Carlo estimation of logical error rates and noise-adapted decoding, and its parameters can be analytically related to the underlying physical noise parameters to enable approximate strong simulation.

Read original at arXiv Quantum Physics
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum physicists Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard win $1m Turing Award
item.description:
Curator's Take: This recognition marks a historic moment for quantum computing as the field's foundational pioneers finally receive computing's highest honor. Bennett and Brassard's groundbreaking work on quantum cryptography, particularly the BB84 protocol they developed in 1984, essentially created the field of quantum information security and demonstrated that quantum mechanics could provide unbreakable communication channels. Their theoretical framework has not only inspired decades of quantum research but is now being implemented in real-world quantum key distribution systems by companies like ID Quantique and Toshiba, making this Turing Award a well-deserved celebration of work that bridged pure physics and practical cryptography. The award signals quantum computing's maturation from exotic physics experiment to a recognized pillar of computer science with transformative potential.

Duo bag award often described as the “Nobel Prize in Computing” The post Quantum physicists Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard win $1m Turing Award appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: International scientists head into the fast-lane of Denmark’s burgeoning quantum ecosystem
item.description:
Curator's Take: Denmark is emerging as a significant player in the global quantum talent race, strategically positioning itself to compete with quantum powerhouses like the US, China, and other European nations. This influx of international scientists signals that smaller countries can build competitive quantum ecosystems through focused investment and smart policy frameworks, rather than relying solely on massive funding scales. The trend reflects a broader shift where quantum expertise is becoming increasingly distributed globally, creating new opportunities for international collaboration and potentially accelerating overall progress in the field. Denmark's success in attracting top talent could serve as a blueprint for other nations looking to establish their own quantum research hubs and avoid being left behind in this critical technology race.

Ambition and international talent converge as Denmark scales up in quantum science The post International scientists head into the fast-lane of Denmark’s burgeoning quantum ecosystem appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Single metasurface could generate record numbers of trapped neutral atoms
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in metasurface technology represents a crucial step toward scaling neutral atom quantum computers to unprecedented sizes, potentially enabling systems with over 100,000 qubits compared to today's few-hundred-qubit systems. Neutral atoms have emerged as one of the most promising platforms for large-scale quantum computing because they can be precisely positioned and controlled using optical tweezers, but generating and trapping massive numbers of atoms simultaneously has been a major bottleneck. The ability to create such vast arrays of trapped atoms using a single metasurface could dramatically simplify the optical complexity while boosting the qubit count needed for fault-tolerant quantum computing. This development positions neutral atom systems to potentially leapfrog other quantum computing platforms in the race to achieve the millions of qubits ultimately required for practical quantum advantage in real-world applications.

Technique boosts prospects for building quantum computers with more than 100,000 qubits The post Single metasurface could generate record numbers of trapped neutral atoms appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum computers must overcome major technical hurdles before tackling quantum chemistry problems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article tackles one of quantum computing's most hyped applications - quantum chemistry simulation - with a much-needed dose of scientific realism. While the quantum advantage for molecular simulation has been promised for years, this Physical Review B study provides crucial benchmarking that reveals the substantial technical hurdles still standing between current quantum hardware and meaningful chemical discoveries. The research is particularly valuable because it compares different algorithmic approaches, giving the field a clearer roadmap of what improvements in error rates, qubit counts, and gate fidelities will actually be needed to surpass classical methods. This kind of rigorous feasibility analysis is essential for setting realistic expectations and guiding hardware development priorities as the field matures beyond proof-of-concept demonstrations.

Although the potential applications of quantum computing are widespread, a new feasibility study suggests quantum computers still face major hurdles in solving quantum chemistry problems. The study, published in Physical Review B, evaluates what criteria are needed for a quantum advantage in searching for the ground state energy of molecules. The researchers attempt this feat using two different algorithms with differing strengths and weaknesses.

Read original at Phys.org Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: IonQ | IonQ & University of Cambridge Launch Quantum Innovation Centre
item.description:
Curator's Take: This partnership marks a significant expansion of IonQ's global footprint, bringing their advanced trapped-ion quantum systems to one of Europe's premier research institutions. The deployment of a 256-qubit system at Cambridge represents a substantial leap in quantum computing accessibility for UK researchers, potentially accelerating breakthroughs in quantum algorithms, error correction, and applications across multiple disciplines. This collaboration positions the UK as a stronger player in the global quantum race while giving Cambridge researchers hands-on access to cutting-edge quantum hardware that could drive both fundamental research and practical quantum applications. The timing is particularly strategic as governments worldwide are investing heavily in quantum technologies, and this centre could become a crucial hub for training the next generation of quantum scientists and engineers.

IonQ partners with the University of Cambridge to deploy a 256-qubit quantum system and launch a new Innovation Centre to accelerate UK quantum research.

Read original at IonQ Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Physicists identify unexpected quantum advantage in a permutation parity task
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research adds another concrete example to the growing catalog of quantum advantages, specifically in solving permutation parity problems where quantum systems can outperform classical computers in determining whether a sequence of operations results in an even or odd number of swaps. What makes this discovery particularly intriguing is that it was unexpected - the researchers weren't initially looking for quantum advantage in this specific mathematical problem, suggesting there may be many more such advantages waiting to be discovered in seemingly mundane computational tasks. This type of fundamental research is crucial for the quantum computing field because it helps map out the true boundaries of where quantum mechanics provides computational benefits, potentially revealing new applications beyond the well-known problems like factoring and optimization. The finding reinforces that we're still in the early stages of understanding quantum computing's full potential, with surprises likely lurking in mathematical corners we haven't thoroughly explored yet.

The list of things that quantum features enable us to do just got a little longer The post Physicists identify unexpected quantum advantage in a permutation parity task appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum memories could help make long-baseline optical astronomy a reality
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in single-photon interferometry over 1.5 km distances represents a crucial step toward revolutionary quantum-enhanced telescopes that could dramatically improve astronomical observations. By using quantum memories to store and synchronize single photons across long baselines, astronomers could effectively create telescope arrays with unprecedented resolution, potentially revealing details of distant celestial objects that are currently impossible to observe. The achievement demonstrates how quantum technologies are moving beyond computing applications to transform fundamental scientific instruments, with quantum memories serving as the critical component that preserves the delicate quantum states needed for ultra-precise measurements. This work bridges quantum information science with astronomy, opening pathways for space-based quantum telescope networks that could revolutionize our understanding of the universe.

Single-photon interferometry achieved over 1.5 km The post Quantum memories could help make long-baseline optical astronomy a reality appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Read-out of Majorana qubits reveals their hidden nature
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough in reading out Majorana qubits represents a crucial step toward realizing their theoretical promise as exceptionally stable quantum computing platforms, as these exotic quasi-particles are naturally protected from many sources of quantum decoherence that plague conventional qubits. The successful demonstration of readout mechanisms brings Majorana-based quantum computers closer to practical reality, potentially offering a path to quantum systems that could maintain coherence for much longer periods without complex error correction overhead. However, the scalability challenges highlighted in the research underscore that while Majorana qubits may solve some fundamental problems in quantum computing, significant engineering hurdles remain before they can compete with more mature qubit technologies like superconducting circuits or trapped ions. This work adds important momentum to the ongoing race to identify which qubit platform will ultimately prove most viable for large-scale quantum computing applications.

Mechanism could pave the way for more robust quantum computation, but questions remain over scalability The post Read-out of Majorana qubits reveals their hidden nature appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum-secure Internet expands to citywide scale
item.description:
Curator's Take: This milestone represents a crucial step toward practical quantum-secure communications, as device-independent quantum key distribution over 100 kilometers demonstrates that quantum encryption can now protect entire metropolitan areas without relying on trust in the hardware itself. Unlike traditional quantum cryptography that assumes devices work perfectly, this device-independent approach maintains security even if the quantum hardware is compromised or imperfect, addressing one of the biggest vulnerabilities in real-world deployments. The citywide scale achievement brings us significantly closer to quantum-secured internet infrastructure that could protect everything from financial transactions to government communications across major urban centers. This breakthrough bridges the gap between laboratory demonstrations and the practical quantum internet networks that cybersecurity experts have been anticipating as essential protection against future quantum computer attacks.

Device-independent quantum-encrypted keys distributed over 100 km The post Quantum-secure Internet expands to citywide scale appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Quantum Systems Accelerator focuses on technologies for computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This podcast episode with Bert de Jong offers valuable insights into the Quantum Systems Accelerator, a major DOE-funded initiative that's working to bridge the gap between quantum research and practical quantum computing applications. As co-director of this multi-institutional effort, de Jong brings a unique perspective on how national laboratories are coordinating to tackle some of quantum computing's most pressing technical challenges, from error correction to quantum algorithms. The QSA represents one of the largest coordinated pushes in quantum computing research, making de Jong's insights particularly relevant for understanding how policy and funding decisions are shaping the field's trajectory. This conversation likely provides a rare behind-the-scenes look at how quantum computing research is being prioritized and organized at the national level.

Bert de Jong of Lawrence Berkeley National Lab is our podcast guest The post Quantum Systems Accelerator focuses on technologies for computing appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Entanglement reveals the difficulty of computational problems
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article explores a fascinating connection between quantum entanglement and computational complexity theory, suggesting that the amount of entanglement present in a quantum system can serve as a diagnostic tool for determining how difficult a computational problem will be to solve. The research builds on growing evidence that entanglement isn't just a curious quantum phenomenon, but a fundamental resource that governs the boundary between problems that are efficiently solvable and those that remain computationally intractable. This work could have significant implications for algorithm design and quantum advantage demonstrations, as it provides researchers with a new lens for understanding when quantum computers will outperform classical ones. The findings also deepen our theoretical understanding of how quantum mechanics fundamentally changes the landscape of what's computable, potentially guiding the development of more efficient quantum algorithms.

Efficiency and hardness of a computation problem are revealed The post Entanglement reveals the difficulty of computational problems appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Metasurfaces create super-sized neutral atom arrays for quantum computing
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough represents a major scaling milestone for neutral atom quantum computing, potentially enabling systems with over 100,000 qubits compared to today's typical arrays of hundreds to thousands of atoms. Metasurfaces offer precise optical control over massive atomic arrays by manipulating light at the nanoscale, providing the kind of scalable architecture that neutral atom platforms need to compete with superconducting and trapped ion systems. The ability to create such large, well-controlled atomic arrays could accelerate the timeline for fault-tolerant quantum computing, as neutral atoms already demonstrate some of the longest coherence times and highest gate fidelities in the field. This development positions neutral atom quantum computers as serious contenders for achieving the scale needed for practical quantum advantage in optimization and simulation problems.

Technique lays the groundwork for neutral-atom quantum computers with more than 100,000 qubits, say physicists The post Metasurfaces create super-sized neutral atom arrays for quantum computing appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Joined-up thinking in quantum metrology: why collaboration is the secret of success
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article highlights a crucial but often overlooked aspect of the quantum revolution: the urgent need for international standards as quantum technologies transition from lab curiosities to real-world applications. The National Physical Laboratory's collaboration with other metrology institutes represents a critical foundation-laying effort that will determine how quantum sensors, clocks, and other precision instruments can be reliably compared and validated across borders. Without these collaborative standards frameworks, the quantum advantage in sensing and timing could become fragmented into incompatible national silos, potentially slowing adoption in everything from GPS improvements to medical diagnostics. This behind-the-scenes work may not grab headlines like quantum computing breakthroughs, but it's essential infrastructure that will enable the practical quantum technologies of tomorrow.

NPL has joined forces with other leading National Metrology Institutes to shape the international standards effort in quantum technologies The post Joined-up thinking in quantum metrology: why collaboration is the secret of success appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Could silicon become the bedrock of quantum computers?
item.description:
Curator's Take: Silicon Quantum Computing's demonstration of an 11-qubit processor represents a significant milestone for silicon-based quantum computing, potentially offering a pathway to leverage the massive infrastructure and expertise of the existing semiconductor industry. While 11 qubits may seem modest compared to IBM's recent 1000+ qubit superconducting systems, silicon quantum dots could prove more scalable in the long run due to their compatibility with conventional CMOS fabrication processes. This approach faces unique challenges around qubit coherence and control precision, but the prospect of manufacturing quantum processors in existing silicon fabs could dramatically accelerate the timeline to commercially viable quantum computers. The work highlights silicon's emergence as a serious contender alongside superconducting circuits and trapped ions in the race to build practical quantum machines.

Australian spin-out Silicon Quantum Computing makes the case with a modality-leading 11-qubit processor The post Could silicon become the bedrock of quantum computers? appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Encrypted qubits can be cloned and stored in multiple locations
item.description:
Curator's Take: This breakthrough challenges one of quantum mechanics' most fundamental principles - the no-cloning theorem - by demonstrating that encrypted quantum information can indeed be duplicated and distributed across multiple locations. The research represents a crucial step toward building a robust quantum internet infrastructure, where quantum data needs to be reliably transmitted and stored without losing its delicate quantum properties. By enabling secure replication of quantum states through encryption, this work could solve major practical challenges in quantum networking, potentially allowing for quantum error correction across distributed systems and more resilient quantum communication protocols. The elegant solution opens new possibilities for scaling quantum networks beyond current limitations while maintaining the security advantages that make quantum communication so promising.

“Elegant” result has implications for a quantum internet The post Encrypted qubits can be cloned and stored in multiple locations appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Powerful new developer tools increase the versatility of the Microsoft Quantum platform
item.description:
Curator's Take: This article signals Microsoft's continued push to make quantum computing more accessible to mainstream developers, addressing one of the field's biggest bottlenecks: the steep learning curve for programming quantum systems. By expanding their developer toolkit, Microsoft is positioning Azure Quantum to compete more effectively with IBM's Qiskit and Google's Cirq platforms in the race to build the most user-friendly quantum development environment. These infrastructure investments are crucial because the quantum advantage will ultimately depend not just on hardware breakthroughs, but on having robust software ecosystems that allow researchers and developers to efficiently translate quantum algorithms into real applications. While the summary lacks specific details about the new tools, any improvements to quantum development workflows represent important progress toward making quantum computing a practical reality rather than just a laboratory curiosity.

Microsoft is committed to providing the tools that developers and scientists need to enable applications on quantum machines. The post Powerful new developer tools increase the versatility of the Microsoft Quantum platform appeared first on Microsoft Azure Quantum Blog .

Read original at Microsoft Quantum Blog
item.author: Mark Eatherly

item.title: Modelling wavefunction collapse as a continuous flow yields insights on the nature of measurement
item.description:
Curator's Take: This research tackles one of quantum mechanics' most fundamental puzzles by modeling wavefunction collapse as a continuous process rather than an instantaneous event, potentially offering new insights into what actually happens during quantum measurement. By using a quantum state diffusion framework to characterize measurement in terms of entropy production, the work provides a fresh mathematical perspective on the mysterious boundary between quantum superposition and classical reality. While primarily theoretical, this approach could eventually inform the design of more precise quantum sensors and measurement protocols, since understanding the fundamental nature of quantum measurement is crucial for optimizing any quantum sensing application. The research represents an important step toward resolving the long-standing measurement problem that has puzzled physicists since the early days of quantum theory.

Quantum state diffusion framework makes it possible to characterize quantum measurement in terms of entropy production The post Modelling wavefunction collapse as a continuous flow yields insights on the nature of measurement appeared first on Physics World .

Read original at Physics World - Quantum
item.author: Mark Eatherly
